GARRISON PAID ONE WAY

All communications for insertion in the

Gaelic Athlete should reach the Office, 20

Upper Liffey St., Dublin, at the first post on

Tuesday morning at least. Pen names can be

used, but real names and addresses should in

all cases be enclosed in a postage stamp.

Correspondence and reports are invited on matters

of G.A.A. interest.

Postal subscription rates:- Yearly, 6d 6d ; Half-Yearly, 3d 2d ; Quarterly, 1s 8d.

By means of our "Direct Supply" scheme of dis-

tribution (which permits of the paper being easily

sold where it is not available through ordinary

channels), the clubs are enabled to obtain the

paper direct for their club members, postage free.

Particulars will be supplied on request of a postcard.

August 21, 1915

Ireland's Record in Athletic Sports

From the Tailtean Games to the Present Time

Including the Complete History of the Rise and Development of the G.A.A.

By "VIGILANT."

XXVIII. A MAN OF MARK

MID all the political excitement that perturbed the country, a provoking conflict of opinion that was causing disturbing ripples within the Gaelic Athletic Association the proposed "invasion of America" by a representative army of Gaelic athletes was kept well before the country. The athletes of Ireland and other supporters felt that a serious injury had been done to the prestige of the country, and the home of a race so famous for physical efficiency by the bogus team of athletes brought out west by Fred Gallagher in 1885, and the only way to repair the damage was to send out a great all round group of genuine Gaelic athletes. Following on the letter from Pat Davin and Cusack's com-

![Picture 1]

Dear Sir - In reference to your suggestion to organize a Gaelic Athletic Association to cross the Atlantic to try conclusions with our American cousins, I have been speaking with the members of the athletic and representative men, all of whom approve of the project. As you are aware, I am now too old to train with any chance of success in any favourite line, but there is another work to be done. Mr. Fraher, or Dan Fraher as he is popularly alluded to, is the most virile link between the athletic that was racy of the soil in pre-G.A.A. days, and Irish athletics as developed by the national sporting organization of which he was one of the pioneers.

Dan Fraher was born on November 8, 1853, at St. Anselm's, in the heart of Slabha G.C.S., one of the most historic districts in West Waterford, and to which I have already made some reference. He was a man of many traditions, history, and song, recounting the glories of sport, and the laudation of muscular prowess. It is a far cry back from the twenty-first century of the legends of O'Gan and St. Patrick's, who give mention to the remembrance of the chace at Slabha G.C.S. and Slabha Grot, but the love of sport and the superiority has lived down through centuries. In such an atmosphere it was but natural that the young boys of the parish should devote their time to the pursuit of exercise. The tongue of the G.A.A. was only understood there, among the vast majority of its inhabitants, and the little boys heard the wonderful feats recorded in songs and tales that had been passed down from time immemorial. They heard of King Aengus O'Donnell, as well as of his fight at the battle of Slabha G.C.S., and of his death in 379 A.D. From his earliest years Dan Fraher inclined towards athletic pursuits. In jumping, running, and weight-throwing he was far in advance of those of his own age, and at the age of 15 years he was a well-known athlete. In June, 1873, at Dungarvan, he won the championship of the county, and in the same year he won the championship of the county. He was also successful in the 3000 yards at 20 feet 3 inches. In June, 1873, at Dungarvan, he won the championship of the county, and in the same year he won the championship of the county. He was also successful in the 3000 yards at 20 feet 3 inches.
